item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is certainly confusing, but I think I understand it, it's well written so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except a photograph doesn't really work with the basic anomaly. It's a transmission frequency, not something physical that reflects light. But yeah, this SCP is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is really good but you have to put a photograph too!! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the 9th iteration is the universe being reset, has the universe been reset multiple times?

I love this one +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations on +1000!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hard to explain why I love this one, but I do.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta pay my respects, this is a pretty genius SCP.

item.title: Happy to see SCP-*** (censored bc spoilers) get some love
item.content:encoded:
(potential spoilers ahead)

+ show block
– hide block

Im really glad someone finally decided to show some appreciation for SCP-***. Taking something hated by many for all the "[DATA EXPUNGED]", and actually making it interesting. Thank you. Im one of those few who actually like SCP-579.

Edited the code block into a collapsible for you. ~Zyn

item.title: Re: The story, told more directly. (My interpretation.)
item.content:encoded: I wish we could rate comments, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it, are you talking about how there are multiple versions of this document? Because I think Eskobar recently made it easier to access the previous versions by using the links provided.

item.title: The story, told more directly. (My interpretation.)
item.content:encoded:
Looking back through the comments, I see quite a few people who, while still enjoying the read, feel lost as to some of the details of what's actually happening. So I wanted to take the time to write what my thoughts were as to what the core of this skip is via a detailed summary.

+ show block
– hide block

Right off the bat, we get a radio-signal from extradimentional entity. They and their species do not have physical forms, but rather exist as pure thought seperated from the physical world in its entirety. Its heavily implied that one of these entities is who we as humans refer to as God - original creator of our known universe. In their world, there was a social heirarchy with God at the helm, but another of their species wanted to have Gods power, and tried to take it for himself. God obviously didn't like that, and so sentenced the other guy (lets call him Satan, since I think it fits the story arc) to eternal torture. And this torture would be broadcast 24/7 to EVERYONE, to warn others not to go down the same path as Satan did.

Now, in our world folks are watching this broadcast. There is a popular theory in psychology that humans are each two parts of the whole — the mental self and the physical self. Satan realized that the humans watching had a (relatively) weak mental self, and kicked that half of them out of their body, taking control for himself. These 8 people, now sharing a single mind, escaped the Foundation and began to spread the 'gospel' of the Satan entity. This proves successful, and many humans become brainwashed.

God is pretty upset that Satan is literally taking his creation away from him, so he comes down to personally stop it. He uses modified signals containing his thoughts to take over as many human as he can, uniting them with His mind - similar to what Satan did to those first 8. The fighting between the two — Gods army of bodies controlled by one mind, and Satan's army of brainwashed zealots — go at it in a war that encompases the entire globe. (At one point, Satan's brainwashing penetrates the Foundation, which results in the iteration that resembles a religious text.)

Unbeknownst to God, Satan himself had died years ago, and it is only a self-perpetuating cycle of brainwashing and zealotry that keeps his army going. This of course means that God's goal in this war can never be achieved, and that the war will therefor can't end until all of mankind has been killed.

The two remaining Foundation personelle who havent been taken over by either side of the battle enact a plan to end the whole thing once and for all. By combining 055 (the anti-meme) and 579 (the super-meme) and looking directly at the result, they create a physical paradox that essentially unmakes reality, resulting in the blank page itteration. Reality then undergoes a hard reset that undoes the paradox and the events leading up to it, leaving behind only the logs of those events in the SCP database. The events DID happen, but they happened in a timeline that no longer exists.

Unfortunately, the current Foundation has declared the statements in those logs to be false, and are making efforts to hide and discredit them. In the meantime, they are set to begin the research that started the eponymous chain of events all over again — likely doomed to continuously repeat history in a massive time loop.

I should note that this is all strictly my interpretation of the events that were presented in this skip. I cannot say whether this is what the author originally intended (unless he/she would like to confirm or deny what I've written here). However, I think it does well to bring the story together for someone who's struggling to understand it. I think it goes without saying that I love this Skip. It is one of my favourites on the site, and I only want to help other people enjoy it more.

item.title: I regret nothing
item.content:encoded:
~ soy un Adidal ~
~ you’re a Ruhar, baby ~
~ so why don’tcha kill me ~

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Link to tophet? That's 089, right? Actually, every mention could use a link for convenience, right? 682, 055, etc.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so I'm tempted to leave this skip alone. Clearly at nearly 1000 upvotes its done its thing well. However, Eskobar gave us their skips to tool around with on the wikiwalk, and there is at least one crosslink proposal I have, so lets rock. Since this article is a great example of RAISA shenanigans, I feel that the RAISA mention on the first iteration linking to SCUTTLE would not go amiss, since the latter is a fantastic tale about that particular faction of the SCP Foundation, and what they do. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

— Maria Jones, Director, RAISA

To this:

— Maria Jones, Director, RAISA

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: UPVOTE 700!
item.content:encoded: My favorite part about this is that Roget's Proposal is (at least effectively) canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah hell, this is really impressive. I give you my +1. Shame you're not around anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I'm gonna use this to lure my boyfriend into reading the site.
+1 of course :)
and the discussion was a fun read as well, with all the background in comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm too tired for this shit. I'll come back tomorrow and figure out what the fuck is going on. I have a feeling you'll be getting a +1 from me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a ride! I'll admit I didn't fully get it until I read the comments, but I'm a sucker for alternate-reality stuff (which this technically might not be, but it definitely reads like it).

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As a point of order, necroposting is not listed in the site rules, though spam is. This specific post I don't think is explicitly spam (as it's a continuation of the joke by Cerxi), but it can be construed as immature and spammy, in the context of the multiple posts. Regardless, please tone down on the immaturity, Doctor Hope.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Doctor Hope, you just made a nigh-contentless reply to a comment almost 3 years old. This is both spam (EDIT: which is against site rules) and necroposting (EDIT: which isn't explicitly against site rules, but it is preferred that replies to old conversations have more content).

I'm seeing a few other posts of yours in Recent Posts which are similarly themed; I strongly suggest you cut it out.

Edited per guidance from Decibelles below.

item.title: Re: User Post
item.content:encoded: Reading that made me stab myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn well this was interesting, really good SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is probably simpler to just look at the page source (+Options->Page Source) for SCP-2998-9.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having spent ages looking for it myself: note that the hidden text does NOT get selected when you either CNRL + a, nor when you manually drag-select all the text on the page. (At least on my current version (v47) of firefox.)

You need to look just above the tags and to the right, where there is a thin grey line, directly above (on my screen) page revision "days ago" brackets. This is actually the text, which you can select and paste into notepad or whatever.

Still as goos as the first time I read it way back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, if you go to the correct page, SCP-2998-9, and view the page source, it is there. You probably are looking at the page with the ListPages module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I learned something today.

(Before anyone tears their hair out looking for articles I've accidentally fucked up: I always hit Cancel when doing this; I never made any actual edits.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most people use "+ options" and "page source". I don't recommend ever editing the page to check content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note for future readers: you may have to use the Edit button on the article to view the page source. I tried to use rightclick -> "View page source" in my browser, and to use select all -> copy -> paste, but neither one gave me any indication of the tiny text for some reason.

You should be looking on the iteration that appears completely blank with just a dash for a title, not the one with the black boxes covering everything or the one with the slightly edited version of the original text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This line was one of the less comprehensible lines from the Lox interview for me. Now that you've explained, your version is easy to understand. I think the snag was that I saw the common phrase "good idea" and tried to build an interpretation including that concept, when the real meaning doesn't involve that at all- those words only appear together because the word(s) that separate them have been left out.

I really like the super-simplified English the Lox host uses, I wouldn't want to compromise that, but maybe a different rendering would make this part easier to figure out. Something like "Idea us live good in you" or "Thing in you good us, idea us live in you" would fit the bill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, hold on, it IS there, on both — it's just right-justified, in addition to being light-grey, at the bottom of the page, and tiny. (Somehow I didn't think to look all the way over on the right, until I went back to see what the formatting error might be, and saw the align-rignt.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is tiny text, so it should be showing up with ListPages. I think there might be a format error as it isn't showing on the page either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AHA! Now I can see the message myself, thanks :).

I wonder how many people miss it because it's not viewable if you use the 'next iteration' links to iterate through the pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, you need to go to the actual page. SCP-2998 just has the ListPages module. "offset8" is SCP-2998-9, because offsets start with 0 and Aelanna numbered the pages 1-10 when she set this up for Eskobar (so the offset will be one lower than the page number).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. I'm getting the black boxes on 7, and the blank page on 8, final revision on 9. (When you click the link in my comment, does it take you to the blank page or the black boxes? For me it goes to the blank page.)

Still can't find the message in the source, even with your instructions, but thanks for letting me know what it says :). I think it does add something, so it's too bad it's so easy to miss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Assuming you mean -9, if you scroll down to the bottom of the page source:

+ show block
– hide block

And it is in the humble opinion of this narrator that strange things happen all the time. And so it goes, and so it goes. And the book says, "We may be through with the past, but the past ain't through with us."

There's nothing but blackboxes on -8 though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some of the comments indicate that the blank page (http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2998/offset/8) has some sort of message on it, but I haven't been able to find it (looking at source, copying and pasting everything into a plain-text editor to check for too-small-to-see text).

At this point, I'm going to assume that the blank page is just a blank page, unless someone can say how to view it as something else.

Love this skip, btw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite article on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorite SCPs because of the concepts it is based on and how wonderfully well they meld (a higher-dimensional radio signal that originates from non-Euclidean space). And besides, the false iterations are funny as well.

I was wondering, could the final iteration be classified as Thaumiel? It assists the Foundation in its goals and is integral to the workings of the Foundation from the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually I figured it out, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hit me up on IM. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone explain how the coding for this works?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… What. *mind blown*

+1 for you, good sir. One of the best SCP's I've read in awhile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pure Genius. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … In the section of heretics, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems wrong to have all those unredacted names. A taboo is being broken, there…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About that hidden text
answer your question?

It's not that the text is hidden per se. It's REALLY really small, near the bottom of the page. Either zoom the hell out of your browser or select all/copy and paste into notepad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I read it the more I love it. We need more big ambitious stories like this on the site. Reminds me of my favorite (093) in that way. I especially love one that makes me think before everything clicks. Just the kind of thing that compelled me to join to upvote.
I still do wonder what that hidden message is supposed to be. There's no invisible text, even in the source code unless it's somewhere unexpected like hidden among menu CSS or whatnot.

item.title: Great Job
item.content:encoded: This is probably one of the best written SCPs I've read in a long while. It's hard enough to create a single SCP page. You successfully made 7 for a single SCP. Well done, you deserve a cookie and a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did they remember 055 existed long enough to do anything with it?

I really like most of this, but I don't like the use of 055 and 579. Not just because it's a little silly, but because I don't think an article should rely on understanding of two others and knowledge of an easy to miss line in one of the 001 proposals. No vote for now.

Also, can anyone tell me how to access whatever the hidden message in iteration 9 is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you ate my brain.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the execution is better than the idea +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are just too many iterations to this, and the true meaning is too buried. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ this conversation must be very entertaining for people who are trying to avoid spoilers...
- boomp

Thanks for the explanation! The record lock being an intentional effect of the "reset button" combo is something that seems totally reasonable, so I may headcanon that and call it a day. 'Tis enough to satisfy that last missing bit and calm down the Xaserbrain. :P

It does seem like it would be a nice shotgun approach for collecting any residual memories/etc. of the event, too — any word of random readers claiming that the contents are not fake would be caught and sent up to the relevant authorities for analysis, etc.

Very deep either way. Still like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since you're around, any answer to the earlier question about what happened to the scip's the Lox destroyed? Did the square peg go in the round hole and literally reset time itself a la Doctor Who level Timey Whimey? (I love that autocorrect capitalizes that.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I'm a year late here, but I always have this problem myself since I'm usually on my tablet — click the edit button and you can read the raw code. It'll revel white text, tiny text, stuff like that.

On a regular computer you can just highlight white text and copy paste tiny text. Not that any of this helps if you don't know that there's something there to track down (is technic really annoys me!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers and answers
Hide

Not to get too Homestucky with it, but if you think of the standard timeline of the universe as a record, the reset button scratched the timeline back to the events in 2013 that allowed for the invasion and essentially retarded Foundation research (or at least success in research) into the phenomenon for as long as possible. There are and will be some ramifications from that reset; one of them is that while almost all other indications of the timeline were destroyed, this SCP file can /never/ be completely erased from the archives.

Alternate explanation: the Foundation knows that this is real and really happened and is desparate to see if any personnel were affected in such a way as to give them some insight into the incident, so they leave the file up, presented as "fake", and allow others to be exposed to it as a crapshoot effort to glean evidence into something that happened that is completely beyond their present abilities to understand or counteract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Super-loved this one. +1, etc.

There's still one thing I don't really "get", though. Seeing that the author's been posting about it a bit, I may as well be a jerk n' ask.

+ article spoilers ahead oh no
- womp

It's clear that the idea is that the Adidal incident actually happened and our beloved "square peg/round hole" SCPs served as a reset button, but I'm unsure how the document iterations survived or why they're presented the way they are (e.g. "hey these are allegedly fake, but here's all the real details"). Didn't actually detract from the storytelling, of course; just curious.

I was also about to ask how David figured out that he needed 055 and 579 since they aren't name-dropped in the convo, but then I actually searched for the phrase on the wiki. Clever. Wish I could +2 for that. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not get this at first but then I was like "HOLY SHI-"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I'll just drop some comments in here.
- (hide)

Hmm…. based on some of the other comments you made earlier, I didn't get the feeling that "possessed" was the right word for what Ruhar did to the D-class. I thought you had explained that Ruhar, being of a species that had a dual physical-memetic nature, was able to escape by being an idea in the minds of those "special" D-class who were susceptible to the signal. By having a living idea inside of them, the D-class would become self-motivated (as opposed to controlled, which "possessed" implies) to simultaneously escape, because they all happened to have the same idea at the same time. By being the idea of escaping, Ruhar could get the D-class to escape.

It also seemed that the D-class received the idea of Adidal as well. It's critical that, in your explanation above, "Adidal" is a rank rather than a living entity (i.e., it's an idea only, albeit one that the D-class were apparently not physically capable of dealing with very well).

A couple of things I didn't understand or wasn't sure about:

One, was the theocracy formed as a result of the memetic effects that Adidal and the Lox were able to impose on humanity (or, at least, the people whose brains were susceptible - presumably purging everyone else)?

Two, it was unclear why the iterations in the SCP file survived the restructuring event, as others have mentioned here. Do you have any thoughts about how that happened?

Anyway, in a lot of cases, finding out the "correct" answer (when I didn't get that answer from reading it myself) tends to reduce the fun involved. But in this case, actually having a truer understanding of your intentions reveals a really great concept. Brilliant.

It also shows that sometimes a good SCP comes from having a full story in mind, and then using the SCP as a window onto that story. My failed attempts at writing an SCP involved trying to build the SCP from the inside out, tacking on whatever was creepy or mindblowing, and stopping when there was enough. I think I've learned a lot from reading this (and your subsequent comments). Thanks!
